## 91 
Bhakti in the Form of Remembrance 
Is the Abhidheya 
९१ | तथा ( भा० १२ । १२ ।५६ ) - 
ŚRI SŪTA further said:1 
अहं च संस्मारित आत्मतत्त्वं श्रुतं पुरा में परमर्षिवक्त्रात् । प्रायोपवेशे नृपतेः परीक्षितः सदस्यृषीणां महतां च शृण्वताम् ॥ १५८ ॥ 
I too have been reminded [by all of you] of the Supreme Imma- nent Self of all beings, whom I had heard of earlier from the mouth of the foremost seer, Śri Suka, in the assembly of sages and illustrious beings who were also attentively listening to the discussion while King Parikṣit sat with a vow to fast until death. (SB 12.12.56)2 
एतत्प्रसङ्गेनाहं चात्मतत्त्वमखिलात्मभूतं नारायणं स्मारितः । सम्प्रति परमोत्कण्ठितीकृ- तोऽस्मीत्यर्थः । यदात्मतत्त्वं मे मया महर्षिमुखाच्छ्रुतम् ॥ श्रीसूतः ॥ 
1 
The sense of the verse is as follows: "By the context of this dis- cussion, I too have been reminded of 'the essential Self' (ātma- tattvam), meaning 'the Supreme Immanent Self of all beings' (akhilātma-bhūtam), Bhagavan Śrī Nārāyaṇa. At present, I have become supremely exhilarated in regard to that essential Self (atma-tattvam), whom I heard of from the mouth of the foremost sage [Śrī Śuka]." 
This is the fifth of the five verses cited from Suta's concluding instructions. 
2 aham ca saṁsmārita ātma-tattvam śrutam pura me paramarsi-vaktrat 
prayopavese nrpateh pariksitah sadasy rṣinam mahatam ca śṛnvatam 
467 
468 
Commentary 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
WHEN SŪTA GOSVĀMI appeared in the assembly of sages at Nai- miṣāranya, Śaunaka Ṛsi posed six questions to Śrī Sūta. By way of response, Sūta related what he had heard from his teacher, Śrī Śuka. This prompted him to become deeply absorbed in remem- brance of Bhagavan. From this, it is clearly understood that the essence of all the questions and replies reported throughout the text was focused exclusively on Bhagavan. This implies that the abhidheya described in Śrīmad Bhagavata is nothing other than bhakti, characterized by hearing, glorification, and remembrance of Bhagavan. 
19 Other Dialogues 
Anucchedas 92-94 
## 92 
Bhakti Is the Essential Teaching throughout Śrīmad Bhāgavata 
९२ । तदेवमस्मिन् श्रीमति महापुराणे गुरुशिष्यभावेन प्रवृत्तानामुपदेशशिक्षावाक्येषु भक्तेरेवाभिधेयत्वं साधितम् । तथा ( भा० १।१६।६ ) - 
— 
THUS, in this beautiful Mahāpurāṇa, in the statements of both tutelage and apprenticeship that are exchanged in the rela- tion between teacher and student, bhakti alone has been estab- lished as the abhidheya, or the prescribed means of ultimate attainment. This is again indicated in the following verse: 
तत् कथ्यतां महाभाग यदि कृष्णकथाश्रयम् । अथवास्य पदाम्भोजमकरन्दलिहां सताम् ॥ १५९ ॥ 
O greatly fortunate one [Sūta Gosvāmi], please narrate these incidents if they pertain to Bhagavan Krsna or His devotees, who lick the honey from His lotus feet. (SB 1.16.6)1 
इत्यनुसारेण सर्वेषामितिहासानामपि तन्मात्रतात्पर्यत्वं ज्ञेयम् । विस्तरभिया तु न विव्रि- यते । अथान्यत्र च तदेव दृश्यते । तत्रान्वयेन यथा ( भा० ६।३।२२) - 
According to this verse, the import of all the accounts found throughout Srimad Bhagavata is understood to be concerned with this subject alone [i.e., Bhagavan and His devotees, because all discussions are narrated in response to the above request]. 
1 
tat kathyatām mahabhaga yadi kṛṣṇa-kathāśrayam 
athavasya padambhoja-makaranda-lihām satam 
471I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
As this topic alone would extensively increase the size of this book, I will not elaborate on it. 
The point that bhakti alone is the abhidheya in Śrimad Bhāgavata is evidenced in other dialogues as well. In this regard, we will now examine a number of verses where this is pointed out by anvaya, or positive concomitance. Yama informed his servants: 
एतावानेव लोकेऽस्मिन् पुंसां धर्मः परः स्मृतः । भक्तियोगो भगवति तन्नामग्रहणादिभिः ।। १६० ॥ 
In this world, this alone is asserted to be the supreme essential duty (para-dharma) of humanity, namely, to engage in bhakti- yoga to Bhagavan by uttering His names and other such direct acts of devotion. (SB 6.3.22)2 
पुंसां जीवमात्राणां परो धर्मः सार्वभौमो धर्म एतावानेव स्मृतः । नैतदधिकः । एताव- त्त्वमेवाह–तन्नामग्रहणादिभिर्यो भक्तियोगः साक्षाद् भक्तिरिति । एवकारेणान्यव्यावृ- त्तत्वं स्पष्टयति – भगवतीति । नामग्रहणादीन्यपि यदि कर्मादौ तत्सागुण्याद्यर्थं प्रयु- ज्यन्ते तदा तस्य परत्वं नास्ति । तुच्छफलार्थं प्रयुक्तत्वेन तदपराधादित्यर्थः । तथैव क्षयिष्णुफलदातृत्वं च भवतीति भावः ॥ श्रीयमः स्वभटान् ॥ 
The word puṁsām, "of humanity," means, "of all living beings" (jīva-mātrāṇām), and para-dharmaḥ, "the supreme essential duty," means, "the universal duty" (sarvabhauma-dharmaḥ). The clause, "this limit, or extent (etāvān), has been asserted (smrtah) as the supreme essential duty of humanity," indicates that there is no higher dharma beyond this. The para-dharmaḥ being referred to by the words etävän eva, "this extent alone," is direct (sākṣāt) bhakti-yoga, consisting of devotional acts such as uttering the names of Bhagavan. By the emphatic particle eva, "only," used to qualify the pronoun etävän, "this extent," Yama denies the possibility of any other dharma being admitted as supreme. He clarifies this by the word bhagavati, "to Bha- gavan." [In other words, the supreme dharma involves those devotional acts that are meant exclusively for the pleasure of Bhagavan.] 
2 
etävän eva loke'smin pumsam dharmah parah smṛtah bhakti-yogo bhagavati tan-nama-grahaṇādibhiḥ 
472 
92 Bhakti Is the Essential Teaching throughout Srimad Bhagavata 
If the uttering of Bhagavan's names and the other acts of devo- tion are applied in regard to karma, [jñāna,] or other practices, in order to bring them to fruition, they can no longer be con- sidered as supreme (para). Rather, when these acts are applied in this way, it is considered an offense to bhakti, because they are then utilized in pursuit of insignificant fruits. In such a case, these devotional practices are relegated to the bestowal of ephemeral fruits. This is the import. 
Commentary 
THE THREE principal topics of discussion in the Six Sandarbhas are sambandha, "a discursive analysis of the Reality denoted by the text of Srimad Bhāgavata," abhidheya, "the means of attainment of that Reality," and prayojana, "the end state to be attained in regard to that Reality." The topic of sambandha was examined in great detail in the first four volumes. The present book, Bhakti Sandarbha, is concerned with an exposition of the means (abhidheya). In the first volume, Tattva Sandarbha, Śrī Jiva Gosvāmī first dealt with the topic of epistemology (pramāna), establishing Śrimad Bhagavata as the supreme authority for deriving valid conclusions regarding the three primary topics with which the anthology is concerned. 
On this foundation, Śrī Jiva began in this book to analyze the dialogues between the principal teachers and students described in Śrimad Bhāgavata in order to objectively ascertain the abhidheya. In doing so, he surveyed the entire book, canto by canto, begin- ning with the first. The unmistakable conclusion made evident through this investigation is that all the major teachers, who are renowned sages within the Puranic tradition, recommend bhakti as the abhidheya. 
Śrī Jiva undertook this elaborate study, which is continued in the upcoming anucchedas, because before his time most of the ear- lier scholars and philosophers across the spectrum of Indian reli- gion thought of bhakti only as a means to mukti. This view is still prevalent. Even among the Vaisnava Schools, there are many who consider bhakti primarily as a means to attain mukti in the form of 
473 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
eternal residence in the transphenomenal abode of Vaikuntha. As a consequence of this predetermined bias in view, it was exceed- ingly rare for someone to recognize bhakti as an end unto itself. To uproot this misconception and to establish bhakti in its own right, Śrī Jiva initiated an extensive analysis of Srimad Bhagavata, the sup- reme pramāņa, or authority, in the matter of the self-disclosure of Ultimate Reality as Bhagavan. 
A list of the significant dialogues examined up to this point in the text is given below: 
1. Suta Gosvāmi offers instruction to Sage Saunaka in 
Anucchedas 3-22. 
2. Śrī Narada instructs Vyasa in Anuccheda 23. 
3. Sri Suka instructs King Parīkṣit in Anucchedas 24-32. 
4. Śrī Saunaka responds to Suta's teachings in Anucchedas 33-40. 5. Brahma instructs Narada in Anucchedas 41-42. 
6. Vidura inquires from Maitreya in Anuccheda 43- 
7. Maitreya cites the prayers of the devas known as Aja and Anaja 
to Kāranodakaśayi Viṣṇu in Anuccheda 44. 
8. Maitreya addresses Vidura in Anuccheda 45. 
9. Bhagavan Kapila instructs Devahuti in Anucchedas 46-47. 10. Sanat-kumāra instructs King Prthu in Anuccheda 48. 
11. Maitreya instructs Vidura in Anuccheda 49. 
12. Rudra instructs the Pracetas in Anuccheda 50. 
13. Narada instructs the Pracetas in Anucchedas 51-52. 
14. Rṣabhadeva instructs His sons in Anuccheda 53. 
15. Jada Bharata instructs King Rahugana in Anuccheda 53. 
16. Rahugana responds to Jada Bharata in Anuccheda 54. 
17. Bhagavan Sankarṣaṇa instructs King Citraketu in Anuccheda 55. 18. Prahlada instructs his classmates in Anucchedas 55-57. 
19. Nārada instructs King Yudhisthira in Anuccheda 58. 
20. The Nava-yogendras instruct King Nimi in Anucchedas 59-65. 21. Śrī Kṛṣṇa instructs Uddhava in Anucchedas 66-85. 
22. Śrī Suka instructs King Parikṣit in Anuccheda 86. 23. Śrī Sūta instructs Saunaka in Anucchedas 87-91. 
In this analysis, Śrī Jīva Gosvāmī did not refer to the Eighth, Ninth, and Tenth Cantos of Srimad Bhagavata. In this regard, he did not want to further extend the scope of the book, as he mentions in 
474 
92 Bhakti Is the Essential Teaching throughout Srimad Bhagavata 
this anuccheda. In summing up this survey of the major dialogues between teacher and student, Śrī Jīva cites a verse spoken by Sau- naka in which the latter requests Sūta Gosvāmī to relate the story of Kali's chastisement by King Parikṣit. In making this request, how- ever, he stipulates that he is interested to hear only if the narration is related to Krsna or His devotees. This suggests that not only this specific episode but all the other stories recounted by Suta Gosvāmī would be required to meet this same criterion; otherwise, his audi- ence would become indifferent to his speech. From this guiding principle, it is concluded that because the entire Bhāgavata is con- cerned with nothing other than the accounts pertaining to Kṛṣṇa and His devotees, its purpose is clearly devotion to Bhagavan Śrī 
Krsna. 
After concluding this extensive analysis of the message of the Bhāgavata's preeminent teachers, Śrī Jīva refers to other dialogues, such as that of Yamarāja to his servants, in which bhakti is again identified as the abhidheya. In the verse cited here (SB 6.3.22), the indeclinable emphatic particle eva, "only," is highly significant. Its function in Yamaraja's statement is to exclude anything other than bhakti to Bhagavan as the supreme essential duty (para-dharma) for all human beings (pumsām). 
Śrī Jīva remarks that if bhakti in the form of uttering the names of Bhagavan is applied in regard to practices such as karma, then such bhakti cannot be classified as para-dharma, because in this case, bhakti would be relegated to an inferior purpose. Indeed, such a secondary application of bhakti is not pleasing to Bhakti Devi. As a consequence, she would award only the results of karma, which are temporary, but not the eternal fruit of prema for Bhagavan. 
3 
The indeclinable eva can have three different applications: (1) specifying the object itself, anya-yoga-vyavaccheda, in which case its function is to exclude any other object from the reference in question; (2) specifying the object's qualifier, ayoga-vyavaccheda, in which case it excludes the non-application of the qualifier to the object in question; and (3) specifying the action of the verb as it applies to the object, atyanta-ayoga-vyavaccheda, in which case it excludes the possibility of the non-application of the verb's action to the object in question (Kiranavali commentary on Nyāya-siddhānta-muktāvalī, p. 299). In the present verse (SB 6.3.22), Śrī Jiva Gosvāmi takes the first meaning of eva, specifying that nothing other than this limit (etävän eva) can be the para-dharma. 
475 
476 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
In the upcoming anucchedas, Śrī Jiva Gosvāmi continues to cite other positive affirmations (anvaya) of bhakti as the abhidheya. 
## 93 
Bhakti Is the Most Appropriate Path 
९३ | तथा च ( भा० ६।१।१७ ) - 
ŚRĪ SUKA said: 
सध्रीचीनो ह्ययं लोके पन्थाः क्षेमोऽकुतोभयः । सुशीलाः साधवो यत्र नारायणपरायणाः ॥ १६१ ॥ 
In this world, this path [of bhakti] is certainly most appropri ate, because it is the source of all auspiciousness and altogether free of fear [from impediments of any kind]. Those who tread this path are of ideal character (susilah), established in virtue (sadhavaḥ), and exclusively devoted to Bhagavan Nārāyaṇa. (SB 6.1.17)1 
अयं पन्थाः श्रीनारायणभक्तिमार्गः ॥ श्रीशुकः || 
The words ayam panthaḥ, "this path," refer to the path of bhakti to Bhagavan Śrī Nārāyaṇa. 
Commentary 
BHAKTI is the most auspicious path, because it is altogether free of fear from obstacles. Moreover, Bhagavan personally helps His devotees, as confirmed by Śrī Kṛṣṇa Himself: 
1 
sadhricino hy ayam loke panthaḥ kṣemo'kuto-bhayaḥ 
susilah sadhavo yatra nārāyaṇa-parāyaṇaḥ 
477 
2 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
But those who have dedicated all actions to Me, who are attached to Me, and who worship Me, meditating on Me through the yoga of exclusive devotion alone - O Partha, I quickly uplift such individ- uals, whose minds are absorbed in Me, from the ocean of deathly material existence. (GĪTĀ 12.6-7)2 
There is no such help on other paths. On the path of jñāna, which is based on isolationism, there is an absence of compassion, while on the path of karma, in which action is motivated by desire for the fruit, envy of others' attainments is prominent. But on the path of bhakti, there is cooperation and love. This is indicated in the verse by the words susilaḥ, "those on this path are of ideal or gracious character," and sadhavaḥ, "they are established in virtue." 
478 
ye tu sarvāņi karmani mayi sannyasya mat-parah ananyenaiva yogena mam dhyāyanta upăsate teṣām aham samuddharta mṛtyu-samsara-sāgarāt bhavami nacirāt pārtha mayy ävesita-cetasām 
## 94 
Bhakti Is the Fruit of All Scriptural Study 
९४ । तत्रैवान्वयेन सर्वशास्त्रश्रवणफलत्वं सकैमुत्यमाह (भा० ३ | १३ |४) - 
AGAIN, by positive concomitance, Śrī Vidura establishes bhakti as the fruit of hearing, or studying, all scriptures with reference to the a fortiori argument (kaimutya) [lit., the principle of “how much more”]:' 
श्रुतस्य पुंसां सुचिरश्रमस्य नन्वञ्जसा सूरिभिरीडितोऽर्थः । तत्तद्गुणानुश्रवणं मुकुन्दपादारविन्दं हृदयेषु येषाम् || १६२ || 
To repeatedly hear the virtues of those in whose hearts the lotus feet of Mukunda perpetually reside has been rightly esteemed by the wise as the true aim of the extensive effort undertaken by human beings in studying the scriptures. (SB 3.13.4) 2 
पुंसां श्रुतस्य वेदार्थावगतेरयमेवार्थः प्रयोजनमीडितः श्लाघितः । कोऽसौ ? मुकुन्द- स्य पादारविन्दं येषां हृदयेषु वर्तते तेषां तत्तगुणानां भगवद्भक्त्यात्मकानामनुश्रवणं यत् सोऽयमिति । ततः सुतरामेव श्रीमुकुन्दस्येत्यर्थः । एवमेवोक्तम् "वासुदेवपरा वेदाः " (भा० ११२।२८) इत्यादि । “भगवान् ब्रह्म कात्स्न्र्त्स्न्येन" (भा० २१२।३४) इत्यादि । 
1 
2 
Kaimutya-nyaya is a type of logic used to establish a point of far-reaching significance by comparison with a truth of lesser significance, i.e., "If that is true, how much more true must this be?" In the verse quoted above, simply hearing about the virtues of those who keep Bhagavan Mukunda in their hearts is said to be the true aim of studying the scriptures. If this is the case, how much more beneficial must it be to directly hear about the virtues of Bhagavan Himself? Thus, bhakti to Bhagavan is established here as the genuine purpose of studying the scriptures through the kaimutya principle. 
śrutasya pumsam sucira-sramasya nany anjasa suribhir idito'rthah 
tat-tad-gunanusravanam mukunda-padaravindam hṛdayeşu yeşăm 
479 
1 Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 

This alone (ayam eva) has been esteemed (iditaḥ, i.e., slaghitaḥ) as the true aim (arthaḥ), meaning the genuine purpose (prayo- janam), of scriptural study (śrutasya), or in other words, of the effort to ascertain the meaning of the Vedas (veda-artha- avagateḥ), undertaken by human beings (pumsăm). What has been thus esteemed? To repeatedly hear about the distinct virtues - one in essential nature with bhakti to Bhagavan – of those in whose hearts the lotus feet of Mukunda perpetually reside. From this statement, it is all the more self-evident that hearing directly about the virtues of Śrī Mukunda is certainly esteemed as the genuine purpose of studying the Vedas. This indeed was stated earlier - vasudeva-parā vedāḥ, "The Vedas have as their exclusive aim Śrī Vasudeva" (SB 1.2.28). This is further confirmed in the following verse as well: 
After thrice studying and deliberating upon the entire corpus of the Vedas, the venerable Brahma, who was of one-pointed intel- lectual resolve, ascertained through his faculty of discernment that by which love for the Self [Śri Hari] is evoked. (SB 2.2.34)3 
तथा च पाद्मबृहत्सहस्रनाम्नि - 
The same conclusion is expressed in the Bṛhat-sahasra-nāma- stotra found in the Padma Purāṇa: 
स्मर्तव्यः सततं विष्णुर्विस्मर्तव्यो न जातुचित् । सर्वे विधिनिषेधाः स्युरेतयोरेव किङ्कराः ॥ १६३ ॥ 
One should remember Bhagavan Viṣṇu always and never forget Him even for a moment. All the rules and prohibitions of scrip- ture are servants of these two principles alone. (Padma Purana, Uttara-khanda 71.100)* 
तथा च स्कान्दे प्रभासखण्डे लिङ्गपुराणे च- 
3 
bhagavan brahma kartsnyena trir anviksya maniṣayā tad-adhyavasyat kuṭastho ratir atman yato bhavet smartavyaḥ satatam visnur vismartavyo na jātucit sarve vidhi-niṣedhah syur etayor eva kinkarāḥ 
480 
94 Bhakti Is the Fruit of All Scriptural Study 
In the Prabhasa-khanda of the Skanda Purāņa and in the Linga Purana also, it is said: 
आलोड्य सर्वशास्त्राणि विचार्य च पुनः पुनः । इदमेव सुनिष्पन्नं ध्येयो नारायणः सदा ॥ १६४ ॥ 
After churning all the scriptures and deliberating on them again and again, this conclusion alone has clearly come to the fore-that one should continuously meditate on Bhagavan Nārāyaṇa. (Skanda Purana 4.25.35, Linga Purana 2.7.11)5 
अत एव वेदार्पणमन्त्र - 
Therefore, in the mantra that is offered to undertake the study of the Vedas, it is said: 
इति विद्यातपोयोनिरयोनिविष्णुरीडितः । ब्रह्मज्ञस्तपते देवः प्रीयतां मे जनार्दनः || १६५ || 
श्रीविदुरः || 
Thus, Bhagavan Viṣṇu is praised as the sourceless ground of all knowledge and penance. May the worshipful Bhagavan Janar- dana, for whom the knowers of the Vedas undergo penance, be pleased with me." 
Commentary 
IN THIS ANUCCHEDA, Śrī Jiva Gosvāmī demonstrates that it is not only Śrīmad Bhagavata that recommends bhakti as the abhi- dheya but that all scriptures do so. In establishing this conclusion, he first supplies evidence from Srimad Bhagavata and then from other Purāņas. The fact that all scriptures advocate bhakti certainly 
2 
aloḍya sarva-sastrani vicarya ca punaḥ punah 
idam eva sunispannam dhyeyo narayaṇaḥ sadā 
This verse is also found in Garuda Purana (229.1). iti vidya-tapo-yonir ayoni vişnur iditah brahma-jñas tapate devaḥ priyataṁ me janardanaḥ Source unknown 
481482 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
stands to reason, because Bhagavan is the source of all scriptures, and He manifested them simply to disclose the means by which He Himself can be known to humanity. The root cause of the jivas' suf- fering is their beginningless ignorance of Bhagavan. Consequently, the sole remedy for this condition is the knowledge and immediate awareness of Bhagavan. The scriptures are the authentic source of this knowledge, and so the means that they advocate - namely, bhakti - is the real purpose of all scriptures, of all paths, and of all rules and regulations. 
In the next anuccheda, Śrī Jīva Gosvāmi shows that even those scriptures or passages that propound varnasrama have bhakti alone as their ultimate purpose. 
Bhakti Is the Culmination of All Methods 
Anucchedas 95-98 
## 95 
Bhakti Is the Fruit of All Dharma 
९५ । यतो यश्च शास्त्रे वर्णाश्रमाचारो विधीयते तस्याप्यनुपमचरितं फलं भक्तिरेव । यथा ( भा० १० १४७।२४ ) - 
— 
THEREFORE, bhakti alone is the unparalleled authenticated fruit even of the conduct prescribed in scripture for the varnas and āśramas, as Śrī Uddhava informed the Vraja-devis: 
दानव्रततपोहोमजपस्वाध्यायसंयमैः । 
श्रेयोभिर्विविधैश्चान्यैः कृष्णे भक्तिर्हि साध्यते ॥ १६६ ॥ 
Bhakti to Bhagavan Śrī Kṛṣṇa is indeed to be aimed at through charity, vows, penances, fire sacrifices, uttering mantras, study of the Vedas, self-control, and various other auspicious practices. (sB 10.47.24 ) 1 
दानादिभिः श्रीकृष्णसन्तोषार्थैरिति ज्ञेयम् । 
In this verse, it is to be understood that practices such as offer- ing charity are meant to be performed for the satisfaction of Bhagavan Śrī Kṛṣṇa. 
1 
तदुक्तम् "तज् जन्म तानि कर्माणि तदायुस्तन्मनो वचः” (भा० ४ । ३१ ।९) इत्यादि । 
The same idea was also expressed by Śrī Nārada: 
dana vrata-tapo-homa-japa-svadhyaya-saryamaih 
śreyobhir vividhais canyaih kṛṣṇe bhaktir hi sädhyate 
485 
1 Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
[In this world,] a human being's birth, actions, lifespan, mind, and speech are successful only if applied in the service of Bhaga- van Hari, who is the Supreme Regulator and the Immanent Self of the cosmos. (SB 4.31.9)2 
बृहन्नारदीये- 
And in the Bṛhan-nāradīya Purāṇa, it is said: 
जन्मकोटिसहस्रेषु पुण्यं यैः समुपार्जितम् । 
तेषां भक्तिर्भवेच्छुद्धा देवदेवे जनार्दने ॥ १६७ ॥ इति । 
Pure bhakti to Bhagavan Janardana, who is the worshipful God of all the gods, is attained by those who have accumulated piety over thousands of millions of births. (BNP 1.39.51)3 
अगस्त्यसंहितायाम्- 
And in the Agastya-saṁhitā: 
व्रतोपवासनियमैर्जन्मकोट्याप्यनुष्ठितैः । 
यज्ञैश्च विविधैः सम्यग् भक्तिर्भवति माधवे ॥ १६८ ॥ इति । 
By perfectly executing vows, fasts, scriptural rules, and vari- ous types of sacrifices for millions of lives, one attains bhakti to Bhagavan Madhava. (Agastya-samhita)+ 
एतदेव व्यतिरेकेणोक्तम् "धर्मः स्वनुष्ठितः पुंसाम्” (भा० ११२१८) इत्यादौ । "यशःश्रिया- मेव" (भा० १२ | १२ | ५३ ) इत्यादौ च ॥ श्री उद्धवः श्रीव्रजदेवीः || 
The same point is made by negative concomitance in the following verses: 
2 
3 
4 
If the conventional dharma prescribed for human beings is meticulously carried out and yet does not induce attraction for 
taj-janma tani karmāņi tad-ayus tan-mano vacaḥ 
nṛṇām yena hi viśvātmā sevyate harir isvarah 
janma-koti-sahasresu punyam yaiḥ samupārjitam 
teşăm bhaktir bhavec chuddha deva-deve janardane vratopavasa-niyamair janma-kotyapy anusthitaih yajñais ca vividhaiḥ samyag bhaktir bhavati madhave 
486 
95 Bhakti Is the Fruit of All Dharma 
the narrations of Bhagavan Vasudeva, such dharma is decidedly only fruitless labor indeed. (SB 1.2.8)5 
And: 
The immense labor expended in discharging prescribed duties pertaining to one's varna and aśrama, and in undergoing penance, study of the Vedas, and other similar practices, cul- minates only in wealth accompanied by fame. But by hearing and repeating the glories of Sri Hari, one attains unbroken remembrance of the lotus feet of Bhagavan, who is the support of the Goddess of Fortune. (SB 12.12.53) 
Commentary 
THE purpose of varṇāśrama is devotion to Śrī Krsna. It is a social system designed specifically to lead all of its members to the path of bhakti. A doubt may be raised in this regard. It has been estab- lished earlier that bhakti ensues only from bhakti itself. Such being the case, how can conventional moral acts, such as charity, give rise to bhakti? Śri Jiva Gosvāmī explains that the varṇāśrama principles can be practiced in two ways. They may be undertaken indepen- dent of Bhagavan, or they may be performed for His satisfaction. 
In the first case, if the varṇāśrama duties are meticulously per- formed, they will yield the aspired for material benefit. This too ultimately occurs only through the grace of bhakti, since the per- former must adhere to the scriptural injunctions, which are the orders of Bhagavan. On this account, Bhakti Devi rewards the per- former for their obedience to the divine ordinance. If, however, one carries out these prescribed acts without faith, and thus with- out authentic adherence to the requisite process, he or she will not obtain even the material result, as attested to by Śrī Kṛṣṇa: 
6 
5 
dharmaḥ svanusthitaḥ pumsām visvaksena-kathāsu yaḥ 
notpadayed yadi ratim srama eva hi kevalam 
yasaḥ-śriyām eva parisramah paro varnasramacara-tapah-śrutadiṣu 
avismrtiḥ śridhara-pada-padmayor guṇānuvada-śravaṇādirbhir hareḥ 
487 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
O son of Prtha, whatever is offered in the sacrificial fire, whatever is given in charity, whatever austerity is undertaken, and what- ever other auspicious actions are performed without faith, are all termed asat, or worthless, because they bear no fruit, neither here nor after death. (GĪTĀ 17.28)' 
The alternate and superior manner of carrying out varṇāśrama duties is to do so for the satisfaction of Bhagavan. If conducted with this intent, then by the grace of a devotee, these sanctified acts will give rise to a taste for hearing about Kṛṣṇa. This in turn will impel the performer to take to pure bhakti. Only this type of execution of varṇāśrama principles is truly praiseworthy. This is the intended meaning of Uddhava's statement. 
In the next anuccheda, it will be demonstrated that true jñāna ensues only from bhakti. 
7 
asraddhaya hutam dattam tapas taptam krtam ca yat asad ity ucyate partha na ca tat pretya no iha 
488 
## 96 
Authentic Jñāna Ensues Only from Bhakti 
९६ । यच्च तत्र ज्ञानमभिधीयते तदपि भक्त्यन्तर्भूततयैव लभ्यम् । यथा ( भा० १०।१४।५ ) - 
EVEN the jñāna that is prescribed in Śrīmad Bhagavata is to be understood as included within bhakti alone. An example of this is seen in the following prayer of Brahma to Bhagavan Śrī Kṛṣṇa: 
पुरेह भूमन् बहवोऽपि योगिनस्त्वदर्पितेहा निजकर्मलब्धया । 
विबुध्य भक्त्यैव कथोपनीतया प्रपेदिरेऽञ्जोऽच्युत ते गतिं पराम् ॥ १६९ ॥ 
O Unlimited, Infallible Lord, in the past, many yogis in this world offered their prescribed practices and even their worldly actions unto you. As a consequence of thus surrendering their actions unto You, they obtained bhakti in the form of hearing and recounting Your narrations. Then, having directly realized the self by such bhakti alone, they easily attained Your supreme destination. (sB 10.14.5) 1 
हे भूमन् ! इह लोके पूर्वं बहवो योगिनोऽपि सन्तो योगैर्ज्ञानमप्राप्य पश्चात् त्वयि अर्पि- तेहा लौकिक्यपि चेष्टा । तथा अर्पितानि यानि निजानि कर्माणि तैर्लब्धया कथारुचि- रूपया पुनश्च कथोपनीतया कथया त्वत्समीपं प्रापितया कथनीयरुचिरूपया भक्त्यैव अञ्जः सुखेन विबुध्य आत्मतत्त्वमारभ्य श्रीभगवत्तत्त्वपर्यन्तमनुभूय तव परमान्तरङ्गां गतिं प्राप्ताः । 
1 
pureha bhuman bahavo'pi yoginas tvad-arpitehä nija-karma-labdhaya vibudhya bhaktyaiva kathopanitaya prapedire 'fijo'cyuta te gatim param 
489 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
The sense of the verse is as follows: "O Unlimited Lord (bhuman), previously there were indeed many yogis in this world. Hav- ing failed to attain realized knowledge (jñāna) through their practice of yoga, they later offered their prescribed practices and even their worldly actions unto You [the word ihā, 'effort or exertion,' implies both types of action]. As a consequence of thus surrendering their actions unto You, they obtained bhakti in the form of the taste (ruci) for hearing Your narra- tions (katha). Furthermore, on account of the contact (sami- pam, lit., proximity') with such katha, they were led to attain bhakti in the form of a liking (ruci) for You directly, the subject of such narrations (kathaniya).? By this bhakti alone [consisting of these two types of taste], they easily realized the Absolute, beginning from atma-tattva, 'the immediate intuition of the self,' up to bhagavat-tattva, 'the immediate intuition of Bha- gavän.' Having done so, they attained Your supreme intrinsic [i.e., supramundane] destination (paramantarangāṁ gatim)." 
श्रीगीतोपनिषत्सु च “अहं सर्वस्य प्रभवः " (गीता १०।८) इत्यादिभिः शुद्धां भक्तिमुपदि- श्याह (गीता १० | ११ ) - 
In Bhagavad Gitā as well, Kṛṣṇa imparted teachings on pure devotion, beginning with this verse: aham sarvasya prabha- vaḥ, "I am the source of everything" (GĪTĀ 10.8). Thereafter, Bhagavan Kṛṣṇa said: 
2 The compound kathopanita can be broken down into two words: kathā, 
"narrations," and upanita, "brought near." The compound appears in the instrumental case and acts as an adjective qualifying bhakti. Thus, kathopanitaya bhaktya means, "by devotion brought about by hearing katha." Jiva Gosvāmi has explained this in two ways. First, one obtains bhakti in the form of the taste (ruci) for hearing the narrations of Bhagavan, then as a consequence of this, one obtains bhakti in the form of attraction for Bhagavan Himself, the subject of such kathā. 
490 
96 Authentic Jñana Ensues Only from Bhakti 
तेषामेवानुकम्पार्थमहमज्ञानजं तमः । 
नाशयाम्यात्मभावस्थ ज्ञानदीपेन भास्वता ॥ १७० ॥ इति ॥ 
ब्रह्मा श्रीभगवन्तम् || 
Just to bless them, I, situated in their consciousness, destroy the darkness born of ignorance with the shining lamp of wisdom. (GĪTĀ 10.11)3 
Commentary 
IN THE previous anuccheda, Śrī Jīva Gosvāmi made the point that the practices that constitute the path of dharma, or "sanctified action," bear real fruit only if performed for the satisfaction of Bhagavan. Now he goes a step further to assert that any attempt to cultivate jñāna, or "immediate awareness of the self," without bhakti will also end in failure. In the main verse of this anuccheda (SB 10.14.5), Brahmā himself admits that in the past many yogis were unsuccessful in their attempts to attain such realization through their practice of yoga. Being thus frustrated, they later offered all of their actions and aspirations to Bhagavan. 
This attestation serves as a lesson for modern-day practitioners. By offering one's actions to Bhagavan, and by the association of a devotee, a person will obtain faith in the process of hearing about Kṛṣṇa. This will lead to constancy in hearing from realized devo- tees and engagement in sadhana-bhakti. This in turn will purify the heart of all unwanted predispositions and desires (vāsanās). Then, by the grace of bhakti, the practitioner will be blessed with direct experience of Bhagavan. Thus, true jñāna ensues only from bhakti. This is confirmed by Krsna in the verse cited here (GĪTĀ 10.11). Any practice devoid of bhakti is termed as ignorance (ajñānam) by Kṛṣṇa in the following pair of verses: 
3 
teṣām evānukampārtham aham ajñāna-jam tamaḥ nāśayāmy atma-bhava-stho jñāna-dipena bhāsvatā 
491492 
1 Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
Undeviating pure devotion unto Me with exclusive absorption, res- idence in a secluded place, nonattachment to the association of worldly people, constancy in the culture of knowledge pertaining to the Self, and deliberation on the goal of knowledge regarding the Absolute - all this is declared to be knowledge. Whatever is different from this is ignorance. (GĪTĀ 13.10-11)*

## 97 
Bhakti Is the Source of All Perfection 
९७ । यान्यन्यानि सर्वाणि तत्र पुरुषार्थसाधनान्युच्यन्ते तान्यपि तथैव भक्तिमूलान्येव । यथा ( भा० १० । ८१ ।१९ ) - 
ALL other practices (sādhanas) that are described in Śrīmad Bhāgavata as productive of the ultimate good (puruṣārtha) are similarly rooted only in bhakti, as expressed in these words of Śrīdāmā Vipra: 
स्वर्गापवर्गयोः पुंसां रसायां भुवि सम्पदाम् । 
सर्वासामपि सिद्धीनां मूलं तच्चरणार्चनम् || १७१ || 
Worship of Your lotus feet is the root of all perfections and opu- lences that human beings can attain in heaven, in liberation, on earth, or in the subterrestrial realms. (SB 10.81.19 ) 1 
“मन्त्रतस्तन्त्रतश्छिद्रम्” (भा०८।२३।१६ ) इत्यादिन्यायेन "मुखबाहूरुपादेभ्यः " ( भा० ११।५।२) इत्याद्युक्तनित्यत्वेन च सर्वथा तद्बहिर्मुखानां तु तत्तदलाभ एव स्यादित्यर्थः । यथा स्कान्दे- 
1 
[In undertaking the ritual process associated with motivated action (kāmya-karma), many errors can arise in regard to mantra and sadhana.] In this respect, the following principle is enunciated [about bhakti]: 
svargāpavargayoḥ pumsām rasayam bhuvi sampadam 
sarvāsām api siddhīnām mūlam tac-caraṇārcanam 
493 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
Defects pertaining to the pronunciation of mantras, the proce- dure to be followed, the time and location of the ritual, the eli- gibility of the persons involved, and the items employed are all made flawless by chanting Your names. (SB 8.23.16)2 
Additionally, bhakti's perpetuity (nityatva), meaning, its regular compulsory nature, is expressed in this pair of verses: 
From the mouth, arms, thighs, and feet of the Purusa appeared the four respective vocational classifications (varnas) along with the four stages of life (asramas). The varnas were distinc- tively divided into vipra, ksatriya, vaisya, and sūdra accord- ing to the corresponding gunas that determine their natures. Among human beings belonging to these divisions, those who [naively] neglect to worship the Purușa, who is directly the primordial ground of being of all individual beings (atma- prabhavam) and the Supreme Immanent Regulator of all being (Iśvara), or who [knowingly] show Him contempt, are deprived of their status and fall into degradation. (SB 11.5.2-3)3 
Thus, by virtue of the principle that bhakti rectifies all defects as well as by its necessity (nityatva) - shown respectively in the above two verses - it is understood that those whose regard is directed away from Bhagavan (tad-bahirmukhānām), certainly cannot attain their respective aspired for goals, as stated in Skanda Purāṇa: 
2 
3 
4 
विष्णुभक्तिविहीनानां श्रौताः स्मार्ताश्च याः क्रियाः । 
कायक्लेशः फलं तासां स्वैरिणीव्यभिचारवत् ॥ १७२ ॥ इति । 
The actions performed according to Śruti and Smrti of those who are devoid of bhakti to Vişņu result only in bodily misery, like the illicit affairs of a prostitute. (Skanda Purāṇa)*

97 Bhakti Is the Source of All Perfection 
तदुक्तं श्रीयुधिष्ठिरेण (भा० १० ।७२।४) - 
Accordingly, Śri Yudhisthira Mahārāja said: 
त्वत्पादुके अविरतं परि ये चरन्ति ध्यायन्त्यभद्रनशने शुचयो गृणन्ति । विन्दन्ति ते कमलनाभ भवापवर्गमाशासते यदि त आशिष ईश नान्ये ॥ १७३ ॥ इति । 
O lotus-navelled Lord, Your foot sandals destroy all inauspi- ciousness. Those who constantly render [bodily] service to them, who meditate upon them with pure minds, and who praise them [through speech], obtain freedom from bondage to the material world. And if they should so desire, they can attain even material boons. Others, however, cannot attain such things. (SB 10.72.4)5 
अत उक्तं बृहन्नारदीये - 
Therefore, it is said in the Bṛhan-nāradīya Purāṇa: 
यथा समस्तलोकानां जीवनं सलिलं स्मृतम् । 
तथा समस्तसिद्धीनां जीवनं भक्तिरिष्यते ॥ १७४ ॥ इति ॥ 
श्रीदामविप्रः ॥ 
Just as water is said to be the life of all living beings, so too bhakti is acknowledged as the life of all perfections. (BNP 1.4.4) 
Commentary 
BHAKTI is the basis of success in all undertakings, be it karma- yoga, jñāna-yoga, or aṣṭanga-yoga. Even in worldly dealings, peo- ple attain success by polite and humble behavior, not by arrogance or indifference. Even politicians have to make a show of devotion and promise public service to win votes. People support a leader 
0 
tvat-paduke aviratam pari ye caranti dhyayanty abhadra-nasane sucayo grnanti vindanti te kamala-nābha bhavapavargam aśäsate yadi ta ăŝisa isa nänye 
yathā samasta-lokānāṁ jīvanaṁ salilaṁ smṛtam 
tatha samasta-siddhīnām jīvanaṁ bhaktir isyate 
495 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
whom they think will provide services that will be of value to them and their community. If even material success is reliant on a devo- tional disposition, how much more must this be the case in regard to spiritual endeavors? 
Bhagavan is the Supreme Master and Regulator of everything. We have the capacity to act, but the result is not in our control. This is clearly expressed by Sri Krsna in the Gita: "Your eligibil- ity pertains exclusively to the fulfillment of action but never to the fruits thereof" (GITA 2.47)? There are many factors involved in the outcome of actions, such as time and place. Beyond all other material considerations, there is the overseership of the Supreme Immanent Self, Paramātmā. Everything functions under His supervision. Therefore, no result can come about without His consent. Sudama thus concludes that all perfections are rooted in the worship of Kṛṣṇa's feet (SB 10.81.19). 
Śrī Jiva then cites three verses to show that bhakti is a compul- sory duty, without which all other prescribed duties are flawed. The first verse (SB 8.23.16) is spoken by Sukrācārya, the guru of Bali, who was the king of the asuras. Bali's yajña was interrupted by Vamanadeva when He went there to beg land from Bali. Bha- gavan Vamanadeva had requested Sukracārya to complete any deficiency that might occur in the yajña. In response to this pro- posal, Šukrācārya spoke this verse. Sukrācārya declared that all perfection comes about merely by chanting the name of Bhaga- van. Those seeking material rewards alone can obtain the results of their respective undertakings by the grace of bhakti, but if one performs the same actions as an offering to Bhagavan, one will also attain bhakti in course of time. 
The next pair of verses cited by Śrī Jiva (SB 11.5.2-3) is spoken by the sage Camasa. He makes it emphatically clear that without devo- tion to the Supreme Self, who is the very source of all being, nobody can succeed in any endeavor. Indeed, even if one has attained a high position, he will lose it. This is further confirmed by King Yudhisthira (SB 10.72.4). In this manner, because bhakti is shown 
7 
karmany evādhikāras te ma phaleşu kadācana 
mā karma-phala-hetur bhur ma te sangostv akarmani 
496 
97 Bhakti Is the Source of All Perfection 
to be the root of all practices and compulsory for their success, it is thus concluded to be the abhidheya. 
Next, Śrī Jīva Gosvāmi makes the point that bhakti is the life of all other paths, and as such it is not dependent on anything. 
497 
## 98 
Bhakti Is Independent of All Methods 
९८ । तदेवं तानि साधनानि भक्तिजीवनान्येवेति भक्तेरेव सर्वत्राभिधेयत्वम् । तानि विनापि च भक्तेरेव तत्र साधकत्वमपि दर्शितम् तत्र “ अकामः सर्वकामो वा ( भा० २।३।१०) इत्यादौ । 
1 
IT IS THUS evidenced that because bhakti is the life of all these different practices (sadhanas), bhakti alone has been proclaimed everywhere as the abhidheya. It is moreover observed that the aspired for ends of these various practices are brought to fruition by bhakti alone, even without recourse to these practices themselves. This is expressed in the following 
statement: 
A person of evolved discernment, whether desireless, desiring all, or seeking liberation, should worship the Supreme Puruşa with resolute bhakti-yoga. (SB 2.3.10)1 
यथा श्रीविष्णुपुराणे पुलहवाक्यम् (वि० पु० १ । ११ । ४६ ) - 
As the sage Pulaha says in the Viṣṇu Purāṇa: 
यो यज्ञपुरुषो यज्ञे योगे यः परमः पुमान् । 
तस्मिंस्तुष्टे यदप्राप्यं किं तदस्ति जनार्दने ॥ १७५ ॥ 
If Bhagavan Janardana, who is known as "the Lord of Sacrifice" (yajña-puruşa) in the domain of sacrificial action and as "the

tivrena bhakti-yogena yajeta puruşam param

98 Bhakti Is Independent of All Methods 
Supreme Immanent Self" (paramaḥ puman) in the practice of yoga, is pleased, what remains unattainable ? (VP 1.11.46 ) 2 
अत एव मोक्षधर्मे- 
Therefore, it is said in the Mokşa-dharma of the Mahabharata: 
या वै साधनसम्पत्तिः पुरुषार्थचतुष्टये । 
तया विना तदाप्नोति नरो नारायणाश्रयः || १७६ ॥ इति । 
By taking shelter of Bhagavan Nārāyaṇa, a human being attains the four human goals even without recourse to any of the vari- ous practices that are meant for obtaining them. (Mahabharata, Moksa dharma ) " 
तस्मात् साधूक्तं सर्वशास्त्रश्रवणफलत्वेन तदभिधेयत्वम् । अत एव प्रथमं स्वयं भगवता सैव प्रवर्तितेत्युक्तम् "कालेन नष्टा प्रलये वाणीयम्” (भा० ११ | १४ | ३ ) इत्यादिना । 
Therefore, it was rightly said that bhakti is the abhidheya, because it is the fruit of studying all scriptures. For this rea- son, in the beginning of an evolutionary cycle, bhakti alone was brought forth into being by Bhagavan Himself, as was said: 
In due course of time, this speech of Mine, known as the Veda, remained undisclosed during the period of dissolution. In it, the dharma that is entailed is constituted of the very essence of My Being (mad-ātmakaḥ). It was spoken by Me once again to Brahmā, at the beginning of a new creational cycle. ( SB 11.14.3 ) + 
तदेवं सति ये तु नातिकोविदास्ते तत्तदर्थं कर्माद्यङ्गत्वेनैव श्रीविष्णूपासनं कुर्वते । ततस्त- दपराधेन निजकामनामात्रफलप्रदत्वं तत्रानियतत्वं च तस्याः । तत्तदर्थमपि स्वतन्त्रत्वेन क्रियमाणाया भक्तस्त्ववश्यं तत्तत्फलप्रदत्वम् । न च तत्तन्मात्रदानेन पर्याप्तिः किन्तु पर्यवसाने परमफलप्रदत्वमेवेति ।

yo yajña-puruso yajne yoge yaḥ paramaḥ puman 
fasmims tuste yad-aprapyam kim tad asti janardane 
yā vai sadhana-sampattih purusārtha catustaye 
tayă vină tad-âpnoti naro narayanasrayah 
kälena naşta pralaye vaniyam veda-safijfită 
mayadau brahmane prokta dharmo yasyam mad-atmakaḥ 
499 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
As bhakti is so much emphasized, even those who are not overly discerning worship Bhagavan Sri Visņu, yet they do so only as a part of karma, or as part of whatever path they are following, in order to achieve the results of their respective practice. This, however, is an offense to bhakti. As a result, bhakti awards them only with the fruit of their respective desires, and of that also there is no assurance. But, if bhakti is performed independent of those practices, even if only to attain the results proclaimed by those paths, bhakti certainly awards those results. Moreover, bhakti is not limited merely to offering those particular fruits, rather in the end it bestows the supreme fruit as well. 
ततस्तस्या एव परमहितत्वेनाभिधेयत्वमाह (भा० ५।१९।२७) - 
Therefore, bhakti alone is the abhidheya, because it grants the ultimate good. This is affirmed by the devas: 
सत्यं दिशत्यर्थितमर्थितो नृणां नैवार्थदो यत् पुनरर्थिता यतः । 
स्वयं विधत्ते भजतामनिच्छतामिच्छापिधानं निजपादपल्लवम् ॥ १७७ ॥ 
It is true that Bhagavan, when prayed to by human beings, awards the results that they seek. [Yet by doing so,] He does not offer anything of genuine value, because [after the petitioners have enjoyed their rewards,] they again pray for something new. [Recognizing this pattern,] Bhagavan personally offers His own lotus feet to those who worship Him, even though they don't aspire for them, and this brings an end to their material desires. (SB 5.19.27) 5 
अर्थितः प्रार्थितः सन् नृणामर्थितं सत्यमेव ददाति । न तत्र कदाचिदपि व्यभिचार इत्य- र्थः । किन्तु तथापि तन्मात्रेणार्थदो न भवति तन्मात्रं दत्त्वा निवृत्तो न भवतीत्यर्थः । यत उपासकस्तत्रापूर्णत्वाद् भोगक्षये सति तदेव पुनरर्थिता भवति न जातु कामः कामा- नाम्" (भा० ९ १९ १४) इत्यादेः । तदेवमभिप्रेत्य स तु परमकारुणिकस्तत्पादपल्लवमा- धुर्यज्ञानेन तदनिच्छतामपि भजतामिच्छापिधानं सर्वकामसमापक निजपादपल्लवमेव विधत्ते तेभ्यो ददातीत्यर्थः । यथा माता चर्व्यमाणां मृत्तिकां बालकमुखादपसार्य तत्र खण्डं ददाति तद्वदिति भावः । 
5 
satyam disaty arthitam arthito nṛnam naivarthado yat punar arthita yataḥ svayam vidhatte bhajatām anicchatam iccha-pidhānam nija-pāda-pallavam 
500 
98 Bhakti Is Independent of All Methods 
Being prayed to by human beings, Bhagavan truly indeed (satyam eva) awards the results that they seek. The word satyam, "it is true," indicates that this cannot ever be otherwise. Yet, by doing so, He does not offer anything of genuine value, because even after awarding such boons, He is not relieved from their solicitations. The reason for this is that the worshiper remains unfulfilled by obtaining his desired object, and when his enjoy- ment is exhausted, he again prays to Bhagavan for some new object. This pattern is illustrated in the following statement: "The craving for sensual gratification is never extinguished by enjoyment of the sense objects" (SB 9.19.14). 
Recognizing this pattern, the most merciful Bhagavan awards His own lotus feet, which are here described as iccha-pidhānam, that which brings an end to all desires. This means that He offers His lotus feet to the worshiper even though the person doesn't desire them due to ignorance of their sweetness. This is com- parable to a mother's action of removing a chewed piece of clay from the mouth of her child and offering a sweet in its place. 
एवमप्युक्तम् “अकामः सर्वकामो वा ” ( भा० २।३।१०) इत्यादौ तीव्रत्वं भक्तेः । तथोक्तं गारुडे- 
For this reason also, the resoluteness (tivratvam) of bhakti was emphasized in SB 2.3.10, quoted at the beginning of this anuccheda. A similar statement [to that of the devas in SB 5.19.27], is found in the Garuda Purana: 
6 
7 
यद् दुर्लभं यदप्राप्यं मनसो यन् न गोचरम् । 
तदप्यप्रार्थितं ध्यातो ददाति मधुसूदनः ॥ १७८ ॥ इति । 
When meditated upon, Bhagavan Madhusudana [Śri Krsna] awards even that which was not requested, which is exceed- ingly rare, unattainable, and beyond what the mind can conceive. (Garuda Purana 2.234.12) 
na jātu kamaḥ kamānam upabhogena samyati 
yad-durlabham yad aprāpyam manaso yan na gocaram 
tad apy aprarthitam dhyāto dadāti madhusudanaḥ 
501I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
एवं श्रीसनकादीनामपि ब्रह्मज्ञानिनां भक्त्यनुवृत्त्या तत्पादपल्लवप्राप्तिर्ज्ञेया ॥ देवाः 
परस्परम् ॥ 
Accordingly, it is to be understood that even Śrī Sanaka and the other Kumāra brothers, who were established in the imme- diate realization of Brahman (brahma-jñānīs), attained Bhaga- van's lotus feet by their adherence to bhakti [even though they originally did so only to attain Brahman realization]. 
Commentary 
IN THIS ANUCCHEDA, Śrī Jiva clearly demonstrates that bhakti is supremely independent in awarding its results, whereas all other processes are impotent without bhakti. From this, it is under- stood that one should engage only in bhakti, no matter what one's aspired for end might be. This is the import of the akāma verse (SB 2.3.10) spoken by Sukadeva and already discussed earlier (in Anuccheda 31). 
There are three ways to proceed in the attempt to fulfill one's aspiration within the jurisdiction of sastra. The first is to follow some particular process, such as karma, without recourse to bhakti. This strategy will not be successful, because, of its own accord, karma lacks the power of Bhagavan's intrinsic potency, of which bhakti is constituted, and which alone is capable of effecting the result. The second is to follow the process in question while also taking support from bhakti. In this case, the practitioner consid- ers bhakti as subordinate to his chosen path. This approach may, however, yield the desired result, because it draws support from the internal potency through the performer's indirect participa- tion in bhakti. In the case of the karma-marga, bhakti awards the result of the prescribed action so that the performer does not lose faith in sastra. In this approach, however, making bhakti secondary to karma is disrespectful to bhakti. Since bhakti's pleasure is not invoked by such an attitude, she offers only that which is desired by the performer and nothing more. 
502 
98 Bhakti Is Independent of All Methods 
The third and optimal method is to take to bhakti alone, even if one still has material desires. The person who does so does not undermine the value of bhakti, although his seeking of material boons reflects a lack of acumen. Kṛṣṇa not only awards such per- sons the fruit that they desire but He also gives them the experience of Himself. This engenders a sense of detachment from material pleasures, whereupon such a worshiper desists from petitioning for further material boons. This is the meaning of the statement that bhakti gives rise to vairagya as a concomitant effect (SB 1.2.7), cited in Anuccheda 4. 
In SB 5.19.27, the devas refer to Bhagavan's feet as iccha-pidhā- nam - "that which shuts down or places a lid on desires." If it were not so, the devotee would keep on petitioning for one boon after another; material desires are never satiated merely through indul- gence. Bhakti, however, does bring an end to desire, because the bliss derived from bhakti is superior to any material pleasure. This was seen in the case of Dhruva, who worshiped Visņu with the desire to obtain a kingdom greater than that of his father. When, however, Bhagavan Visņu personally appeared before him, Dhruva became fully satisfied and no longer wished for anything else. His testimony submitted at that time to Bhagavan Viṣṇu is reported in Hari-bhakti-sudhodaya: 
Being desirous of a position of great worldly importance, I took to the performance of penance. Though I was searching for broken glass, I have attained You, a divine jewel, who are unobtainable even by the gods and great sages. O Lord! I am now completely fulfilled and ask for no other boon. (Hari-bhakti-sudhodaya 7.28) 
Śrī Jiva Gosvāmi then points out an important connection between the devas' statement in SB 5.19.27 and that of Śrī Suka- deva in SB 2.3.10, with which he began the anuccheda. He writes that the resoluteness (tīvratvam) of bhakti has been emphasized in SB 2.3.10 specifically because of the conclusion drawn by the devas that Bhagavan awards His own lotus feet even to the worshiper 
sthānabhilaşi tapasi sthito ham tvām präptavān deva-munindra-guhyam 
8 
kacam vicinvann api divya-ratnam svamin kṛtārtho'smi varam na yace 
503 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
with desires. The word tivrena in SB 2.3.10 thus signifies that one should wholeheartedly engage in bhakti alone, without depending upon any other process. In other words, one should not think of bhakti as secondary to any other process. Making it secondary is comparable to the sun's brilliance being covered with clouds. By doing so, one will not experience the true potency of bhakti. Dhruva is an example of a person with an extravagant material desire who performed resolute (tivra) bhakti. The Kumāra broth- ers are mentioned as an example of brahma-jñānīs who desired liberation. They too engaged in bhakti and by so doing attained not only mukti but also bhakti. 
Taking all of this into consideration, one should not engage in karma devoid of bhakti, nor in bhakti subordinated to karma, nor even in bhakti alone undertaken to fulfill material objectives. Rather, one should engage exclusively in bhakti and for the sake of the pleasure of Bhagavan alone. At present, the majority of peo- ple do not fit in any of these four categories. They are primarily interested in earning money and enjoying their senses without fol- lowing any sort of spiritual process. Śrī Jīva Gosvāmī does not even bother to speak about them. 
Next, Śri Jiva establishes bhakti as the abhidheya by vyatireka, or negative concomitance. 
504 
Bhakti Established through Negative Inference - Vyatireka 
Anucchedas 99-105 
## 99 
Bhakti Contrasted with Karma 
९९ । अथ व्यतिरेकेण कर्मानादरेणाह - तत्र कर्मणः फलप्राप्तावनिश्चयवत्त्वं दुःखरूपत्वं च भक्तेस्तु तस्यामावश्यकत्वं साधकदशायामपि सुखरूपत्वं चेत्याह ( भा० १११८ ११२ ) - 
NOW BHAKTI is established as the abhidheya through negative concomitance (vyatireka), by expressing disregard for the path of karma. There is both uncertainty and misery involved in attaining the results of karma. But in the case of bhakti, suc- cess is assured and the devotee experiences joy even in the stage of practice. These conclusions are summed up in the following statement of the sage Saunaka to Sūta Gosvāmi: 
कर्मण्यस्मिन्ननाश्वासे धूमधूम्रात्मनां भवान् । आपाययति गोविन्दपादपद्मासवं मधु || १७९ ।। 
We have been engaged in the performance of this sacrifice, the fruits of which are uncertain, until our bodies were blackened by smoke. Now you have come and revived us by causing us to drink the ambrosial nectar flowing from Śrī Govinda's lotus feet. (SB 1.18.12)1 
अस्मिन् कर्मणि सत्रेऽनाश्वासेऽविश्वसनीये वैगुण्यबाहुल्येन कृषिवत् फलनिश्चयाभा- वात् । अनेन भक्तेर्विश्वसनीयत्वं ध्वनितम् । धूमेन धूम्रो विरञ्जितौ विवर्णावात्मानौ शरीरचित्ते येषाम् । कर्मणि षष्ठी तानस्मानित्यर्थः । पादपद्मस्य यशोरूपमासर्व मकरन्द मधु मधुरम् । अत्र सत्रवत् कर्मान्तरं यशःश्रवणवद् भक्त्यन्तरं चेति ज्ञेयम् । तदेवं भक्तिविनाभूतानां कर्मादिभिरस्माकं दुःखमेवासीदिति व्यतिरेकत्वमत्र गम्यते । 
1 
| 
karmany asminn anāśväse dhuma-dhumratmanām bhavan 
apayayati govinda-pada-padmāsavam madhu 
507 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
In this endeavor (asmin karmani), meaning "in this sacrifice" (asmin satre), the outcome is "unassured" (anāśvāse), or "unre- liable" (aviśvasaniya). Because of the possibility of many dis- crepancies (vaigunya-bahulyena) in the performance of sacri- fice, there is a want of certainty in regard to the result (phala- niścayābhāvāt), as is the case with farming. By this statement, the reliability (viśvasanīyatvam) of bhakti is indirectly implied. 
In the compound dhuma-dhumratmanām, the word ātmanām refers to "the body and mind" (sarira-citte), dhumra means "dispirited" (virañjita) and "discolored" (vivarna), and dhuma means "by the smoke" (dhūmena). Thus, the compound as a whole means "to us, who have become dispirited and discol- ored in mind and body due to the smoke." Although ātmanām appears in the sixth case [the possessive case], it is used in the sense of the accusative, [indicating that the sages are the direct object of the verb āpāyayati, “You are causing us to drink"]. In the compound pada-padmāsavam, the word asavam means, "the intoxicating nectar of a flower" (makarandam), which flows from the lotus feet (pada-padma) of Śrī Govinda in the form of His fame (yaso-rupam). This nectar, or fame, is "honey-like" or "ambrosial" (madhu). 
In this statement, it is to be understood that the uncertainty and hardship that is specifically pointed out in regard to the satra sacrifice actually applies to all other forms of karma. Similarly, the certainty and pleasure attributed to hearing the glories of Bhagavan applies to all other types of bhakti. Thus, by negative concomitance (vyatireka), it is understood from Saunaka's state- ment that by the execution of karma alone devoid of bhakti, the performers experienced only misery. 
तदुक्तम् "यशः श्रियामेव परिश्रमः परः " ( भा०१२ | १२ | ५३ ) इत्यादि “ अतो वै कवयो नित्यम् " ( भा० १।२।२२ ) इत्यादि च । 
This same idea was expressed by Sūta Gosvāmī: 
The immense labor expended in discharging prescribed duties pertaining to one's varna and aśrama, and in undergoing 
508 
99 Bhakti Contrasted with Karma 
penance, study of the Vedas, and other similar practices, cul- minates only in wealth accompanied by fame. But by hearing and repeating the glories of Sri Hari, one attains unbroken remembrance of the lotus feet of Bhagavan, who is the support of the Goddess of Fortune. (SB 12.12.53)2 
And: 
For this reason indeed, the enlightened sages, out of supreme delight, continuously engage in bhakti to Bhagavan Vasudeva, which is utterly fulfilling to the self. (SB 1.2.22)3 
ब्रह्मवैवर्ते च शिवं प्रति श्रीविष्णुवाक्यम्- 
In the Brahma-vaivarta Purana as well, Bhagavan Śrī Viṣṇu makes a similar statement to Śiva: 
यदि मां प्राप्तुमिच्छन्ति प्राप्नुवन्त्येव नान्यथा । 
कलौ कलुषचित्तानां वृथायुः प्रभृतीनि च । 
भवन्ति वर्णाश्रमिणां न तु मच्छरणार्थिनाम् ॥ १८० ॥ इति ॥ 
वृषयः सूतम् ॥ 
If anyone seeks to attain Me, they certainly do attain Me. This cannot be otherwise. In the age of Kali, the life and other char- acteristics of the impure-hearted who adhere to varṇāśrama are all futile, but this is not the case for those who have taken shelter of Me. (Brahma-vaivarta Purana)* 
Commentary 
AFTER demonstrating through positive affirmations (anvaya) that bhakti is independent in awarding its result and that other paths 
2 yaśaḥ-śriyam eva pariśramah paro varṇāśramācāra-tapaḥ-śrutādiṣu 
3 
4 
avismṛtiḥ śridhara-pada-padmayor guṇanuvada-śravaṇādirbhir hareḥ 
ato vai kavayo nityam bhaktim paramaya muda 
vasudeve bhagavati kurvanty atma-prasādanim 
yadi mam präptum icchanti präpnuvanty eva nanyathā kalau kaluṣa-cittānām vṛthāyuh prabhṛtini ca bhavanti varnasraminam na tu mac-charaṇārthinam 
509 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
depend upon it, Śrī Jīva now establishes the same conclusion through disaffirmation (vyatireka), by showing the inefficacy of other paths. In this anuccheda, he shows the futility of karma devoid of bhakti. 
Thousands of sages headed by Saunaka had assembled at Nai- miṣāranya to participate in a long-enduring yajña, specified by the name satra. According to Vyasa's statement in the beginning of Srimad Bhāgavata, it was meant to last for a thousand years (SB 1.1.4). The sages, however, were unsure that they would obtain the desired result. On this account, they referred to the sacrifice by the word anāśvāse, or "uncertain." They also found the whole endeavor to be quite troublesome, and thus they admitted that their minds and bodies had become covered in soot from the smoke of the sacrificial fire. It is understandable that their bodies became covered in soot, but how could such a thing be said of their minds? This indicates that they were feeling mental dissatisfaction and uncertainty in regard to the result. In this connection, Śrī Jīva glosses the word dhümra, lit., "smoke-colored," with the two words. viranjita and vivarna. Although both of these words mean "dis- colored," the first word in particular can also carry the sense of "dispirited" or "disheartened," which is perhaps what Śrī Jiva had in mind in supplying two otherwise perfectly synonymous terms. 
The path of sacrificial action (karma-marga) is so precise that if the ritual is not executed exactly as prescribed down to the last detail, it will not yield the desired result. A slight deviation even in the intonation of a single phoneme in a mantra may even bring to bear the exact opposite of what was intended. The story of the demon Vṛtrāsura, who was generated from a dark tantrika ritual, and that of Diti, who contrived to conceive a son who would be able to kill Indra, are glaring examples of the fallibility of karma. In con- trast, bhakti is pleasurable even during the stage of practice, and its result is assured because of the supreme independent power with which it is intrinsically endowed. If there is any imperfection in the course of the performance of bhakti, Bhagavan personally brings about its completion, as confirmed by the sage Karabhājana: 
510 
99 Bhakti Contrasted with Karma 
If a beloved devotee of Bhagavan, who is engaged in rendering ser- vice to the soles of His feet, having given up all other objects of affection, somehow becomes involved in a forbidden act, then the Supreme Master Hari, situated in the devotee's heart, destroys all reactions to such an act. (SB 11.5.42) 
5 
sva-pada-mulam bhajatah priyasya tyaktanya-bhavasya hariḥ paresah vikarma yac cotpatitam kathancid dhunoti sarvam hrdi sannivistah 
511Anuccheda 100 
The Worthlessness of Vedic Karma 
Devoid of Bhakti - I 
१०० । तथा “त्यक्त्वा स्वधर्मम्” (भा० १।५।१७ ) इत्यादिकमनुसन्धेयम् । एवं महावि- त्तमहायासादिसाध्येन कर्मादिना तुच्छं स्वर्गादिफलं स्वल्पायासस्वल्पवित्तादिसाध्यया भक्त्या तदाभासेन च परममहत्फलं तत्र तत्रानुसन्धाय भक्तावेव शास्त्रतात्पर्यं पर्यालो- चनीयम् । तस्मात् तत्तच्छास्त्राणामपि भक्तिविधेयकतत्तदनुवादेन प्रवृत्तत्वान् न वैफल्य- मित्यपि ज्ञेयम् । 
[IN REGARD to bhakti's being established as the abhidheya through disregard for the path of karma,] here is another statement that should be examined: 
Has inauspiciousness (abhadram) ever befallen a person, in whatever circumstance he might find himself (yatra kva vā), who, having abandoned his prescribed socio-religious duty (sva-dharma), takes to the sacred service (bhajana) of the lotus feet of Bhagavan Hari, even if, thereafter, while still in an imma- ture state, he should meet with death or somehow fall away from such bhajana? On the other hand, what value (artha) is gained through adherence to prescribed duty by those who fail to worship Bhagavan? (SB 1.5.17)1 
In this manner, it is understood from examining the various statements in scripture that by karma, which is both costly and 
1 
tyaktva sva-dharmam caraṇāmbujam harer bhajann apakvo'tha patet tato yadi yatra kva vabhadram abhūd amuṣya kim ko vartha apto bhajatām sva-dharmataḥ 
512 
100 The Worthlessness of Vedic Karma Devoid of Bhakti - I 
laborious to perform, one obtains only petty fruit, like pro- motion to the celestial planets, whereas by bhakti, which is accomplished with very little effort and expense, one attains the supreme transcendental fruit. In fact, such a result may be obtained even by a semblance (abhāsa) of bhakti. From this, it is to be concluded that the import of all scripture is in bhakti alone. Therefore, it should also be understood that because all other scriptures also advocate bhakti in a roundabout manner by promoting various other processes, they are not futile. 
किं च (भा० ७।९।१०) - 
Furthermore, Śri Prahlada said to Bhagavan Sri Nrsimha: 
विप्राद् द्विषङ्गणयुतादरविन्दनाभपादारविन्दविमुखात् श्वपचं वरिष्ठम् । मन्ये तदर्पितमनोवचनेहितार्थप्राणं पुनाति स कुलं न तु भूरिमानः ॥ १८१ ॥ 
In my opinion, a dog-eater, who has offered his mind, speech, actions, wealth, and life to Bhagavan, is superior to a brāhmaṇa who, although endowed with twelve exalted qualities, is not devoted to the lotus feet of Bhagavan, whose navel is shaped like a lotus. Such a dog-eater purifies his whole family and not the overly proud brāhmaṇa. (SB 7.9.10)2 
टीका च - "भक्त्यैव केवलया हरेस्तोषः सम्भवतीत्युक्तम् । इदानीं भक्तिं विना नान्यत् किञ्चित् तोषहेतुरित्याह विप्रादिति । 'मन्ये धनाभिजनरूपतपःश्रुतौजस्तेजःप्रभावबल- पौरुषबुद्धियोगाः' (भा० ७/९/९) इत्यादौ पूर्वोक्ता ये धनादयो द्विषड् द्वादशगुणास्तैर्यु- क्ताद् विप्रादपि श्वपचं वरिष्ठं मन्ये । 
2 
Śrīdhara Svāmi comments as follows: "It was said that it is pos- sible to please Bhagavan Hari only by unalloyed (kevala) bhakti. Now in this verse, Prahlada asserts that there is nothing other than bhakti that can be a cause for Bhagavan's satisfaction. The 
viprad dvi-sad-guṇa-yutad aravinda-nābha- 
pādāravinda-vimukhat svapacam varistham manye tad-arpita-mano-vacanehitartha- prānam punati sa kulam na tu bhūrimānaḥ 
513 
I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
immediately preceding verse [SB 7.9.9]3 enumerated the twelve qualities referred to here as follows: wealth, birth in a cultured family, beauty, austerity, erudition, sensory prowess, physi- cal power, influence, bodily strength, industriousness, intelli- gence, and the culture of yoga. Prahlada says that he considers a dog-eater [who is fully devoted to Bhagavan] superior even to a vipra possessing these twelve qualities [but who is not devoted to Bhagavan]. 
यद्वा सनत्सुजातोक्ता द्वादश धर्मादयो गुणा द्रष्टव्याः (म० भा० ३।४३।२० ) - 
"Alternatively, the twelve qualities may refer to those mentioned by the sage Sanat Sujata in the Mahabharata: 
धर्मं च सत्यं च दमस्तपश्च अमात्सर्यं हीस्तितिक्षानसूयाः । 
यज्ञश्च दानं च धृतिः श्रुतं च व्रतानि वै द्वादश ब्राह्मणस्य ॥ १८२ ॥ इति । 
The twelve vows of a brahmana are moral integrity, truthful- ness, sensual regulation, austerity, nonenvy, modesty, toler- ance, freedom from jealousy, sacrifice, charity, fortitude, and erudition. (Mahabharata, Udyoga-parva 43.20) 
कथम्भूताद् विप्रात् ? अरविन्दनाभपादारविन्दविमुखात् । कथम्भूतं श्वपचम् ? तस्मिन्न- रविन्दनाभेऽर्पिता मन आदयो येन तम् । ईहितं कर्म । वरिष्ठत्वे हेतुः स एवम्भूतः श्वपचः सर्वं कुलं पुनाति । भूरिर्मानो गर्यो यस्य स तु विप्र आत्मानमपि न पुनाति कुतः कुलम् ? यतो भक्तिहीनस्यैते गुणा गर्वायैव भवन्ति न तु शुद्धये । अतो हीन इति भावः" इत्येषा । 
"What type of vipra does Prahlada consider to be inferior to a dog-eater? He replies that it is a brahmana who is not devoted to the lotus feet of the lotus-navelled Bhagavan. And what type of dog-eater does he speak of? One who has offered his mind, speech, and so on to the lotus-navelled Bhagavan. The word ihitam means 'actions.' What is the reason behind the dog- eater's superiority? Such a dog-eater purifies his entire dynasty, 
3 
4 
manye dhanabhijana-rupa-tapaḥ-śrutaujas- 
tejaḥ-prabhava-bala-paurusa-buddhi-yogaḥ 
närādhanaya hi bhavanti parasya puṁso 
bhaktyä tutoşa bhagavan gaja-yutha-pāya 
dharmam ca satyam ca damas-tapas ca amātsaryam hris titikṣānasuyāh 
yajnaś ca danam ca dhṛtiḥ śrutam ca vratani vai dvādasa brāhmaṇasya

100 The Worthlessness of Vedic Karma Devoid of Bhakti — I 
whereas the vipra, being excessively proud, cannot even purify himself, to say nothing of his family. The import is that for a person devoid of bhakti, all these qualities are simply a source of pride and not of purification. For this reason, such a person is inferior." [Here ends Śrīdhara Svāmi's comment.] 
मुक्ताफलटीकाच - "द्विषड् द्वादश गुणा धनाभिजनादयः । यद् वा- 
In his Mukta-phala commentary (16.26), Vopadeva also says: "The twelve qualities are those referred to in the previous verse [SB 7.9.9], such as wealth and birth in a cultured family. Alter- natively, they may refer to the qualities mentioned in this 
verse: 
शमो दमस्तपः शौचं क्षान्त्यार्जवविरक्तयः । 
ज्ञानविज्ञानसन्तोषाः सत्यास्तिक्ये द्विषङ्गणाः || १८३ ॥ 
The twelve qualities are control of the mind, control of the senses, austerity, cleanliness, tolerance, simplicity, detach- ment, knowledge, realization, satisfaction, truthfulness, and theism.5 
इत्यप्युक्ता" इत्येषा | 
This is the end of Vopadeva's comment. 
स्कान्दे श्रीनारदवाक्यम्- 
In the Skanda Purāṇa, Śrī Narada makes the following statement: 
5 
कुलाचारविहीनोऽपि दृढभक्तिजितेन्द्रियः । 
प्रशस्तः सर्वलोकानां न त्वष्टादशविद्यकः । 
भक्तिहीनो द्विजः शान्तः सज्जातिर्धार्मिकस्तथा ॥ १८४ ॥ 
Although bereft of noble birth and civility, a person endowed with resolute bhakti, who has subdued his senses, is praised by 
all people, but not a dvija who is devoid of bhakti, even if he has 
samo damas tapaḥ saucam kṣanty arjava-viraktayaḥ 
jñana-vijñana-santoṣaḥ satyastikye dvi-sad-gunah

I Bhakti Is the Complete Abhidheya 
studied all eighteen branches of Vedic knowledge and is peace- ful, highborn, and attentive to his prescribed duties. (Skanda Purana) 
काशीखण्डे च- 
In the Kaśi-khanda also, it is said: 
ब्राह्मणः क्षत्रियो वैश्यः शूद्रो वा यदि वेतरः । विष्णुभक्तिसमायुक्तो ज्ञेयः सर्वोत्तमोत्तमः || १८५ || 
Whether one is a brāhmana, a ksatriya, a vaiśya, a śūdra, or any- thing else, if he is fully endowed with Visnu-bhakti, he is to be understood as the best of all the best. ( Kasi khanda) 7 
बृहन्नारदीये - 
And in the Brhad - naradiya Purāna: 
विष्णुभक्तिविहीना ये चण्डालाः परिकीर्तिताः । 
चाण्डाला अपि ते श्रेष्ठा हरिभक्तिपरायणाः ॥ १८६ ॥ 
Those who are devoid of Vişnu-bhakti, whoever they may be, are proclaimed as candālas, or outcastes, but those who are exclu- sively fixed in bhakti to Bhagavan Hari are the greatest, even if they are cāndālas (Brhad - naradiya Purana 1.37.12 ) s
